name: Algeria
summary: Algeria is named among the significant digital economies that still rely on broader cybercrime, penal, or data protection law rather than a dedicated child online protection framework.
instruments: General cybercrime, penal, or data protection coverage referenced in the brief
evidence: The article explicitly includes Algeria in the large general-law category rather than the dedicated COP categories.
opportunity: Use domestication advocacy to move from tangential legal coverage toward a child-specific framework.

name: Burundi
summary: The brief identifies Burundi as having no identifiable legislation, regulation, or policy framework on child online safety.
instruments: No identifiable framework named in the brief
evidence: Burundi is one of the five no-framework jurisdictions named directly in the article.
opportunity: Link agenda-setting to AU domestication pressure and sub-regional standards.

name: Cameroon
summary: Cameroon is treated as part of the top-tier binding group while the brief also notes that its framework is robust and multi-layered rather than a standalone COP statute.
instruments: Charter on Child Online Protection in Cameroon - Law No. 2023/009 of July 25, 2023
evidence: The brief names Cameroon both in the top-tier count and in the Francophone acceleration pattern.
opportunity: Use Cameroon as a Francophone peer case for sub-regional harmonisation and institutional champion strategy.

name: Cote d'Ivoire
summary: Cote d'Ivoire is named among significant digital economies that still rely on broader cybercrime, penal, or data protection law without a dedicated COP framework.
instruments: General cybercrime, penal, or data protection coverage referenced in the brief
evidence: The brief explicitly includes Cote d'Ivoire in the general-law category rather than the dedicated COP categories.
opportunity: Frame Cote d'Ivoire as a priority domestication target inside broader West African harmonisation work.

name: Djibouti
summary: Djibouti is cited as having adopted a Digital Code in 2025, signalling legislative movement in Francophone Africa without a dedicated COP framework.
evidence: The article uses Djibouti to show that Francophone legal movement is broadening beyond the earlier leaders.
opportunity: Press broad digital-code architecture toward child-specific obligations rather than generic digital governance.

name: Egypt
summary: Egypt is named among the significant digital economies that rely on general cybercrime, penal, or data protection laws with only tangential relevance to child online safety.
instruments: General cybercrime, penal, or data protection coverage referenced in the brief
evidence: The article explicitly places Egypt in the general-law category rather than the dedicated COP categories.
opportunity: Use Egypt as a high-visibility domestication target given its scale and regional influence.

name: Eritrea
summary: The article identifies Eritrea as having no identifiable legislation, regulation, or policy framework on child online safety.
instruments: No identifiable framework named in the brief
evidence: Eritrea is one of five countries the brief places in the no-framework category.
opportunity: Use AU pressure to establish a minimum policy foothold.

name: Eswatini
summary: Eswatini is cited as having five-part COP Guidelines, indicating policy growth without the permanence or enforcement teeth of primary legislation.
evidence: The article lists Eswatini among the jurisdictions expanding the guideline-and-strategy layer rather than the legislative layer.
opportunity: Use the guideline base to press for legislative anchoring and implementation accountability.

name: Ethiopia
summary: Ethiopia is named as a significant digital economy that still relies on broader legal frameworks rather than dedicated COP instruments.
instruments: General cybercrime, penal, or data protection coverage referenced in the brief
evidence: The brief places Ethiopia in the large general-law category and later uses it as a strategic advocacy focus.
opportunity: Use high-level domestication pressure and CRC-linked advocacy to move toward dedicated child online protection coverage.

name: Gabon
summary: Gabon’s 2026 Social Media Age Verification Ordinance is cited as a notable Francophone development on age assurance without full dedicated COP coverage.
evidence: The brief uses Gabon both in the Francophone pattern and the age-verification gap discussion.
opportunity: Push age-assurance debates toward rights-respecting standards, transparency, and child-specific design obligations.

name: Gambia
summary: The article identifies Gambia as having no identifiable child online safety legislation, regulation, or policy framework.
instruments: No identifiable framework named in the brief
evidence: Gambia is named directly in the no-framework group.
opportunity: Build the case for a baseline child-protection and digital-rights entry point.

name: Ghana
summary: The brief cites Ghana as having a Cybersecurity Act with child-specific provisions and a National COP Framework issued in 2024.
evidence: Ghana is one of the jurisdictions the brief treats as having binding child online safety coverage.
opportunity: Use Ghana as a benchmark for institutional mandate, implementation design, and regional peer pressure.

name: Guinea-Bissau
summary: Guinea-Bissau is listed as having no identifiable child online safety framework in the brief.
instruments: No identifiable framework named in the brief
evidence: Guinea-Bissau appears in the article’s no-framework category.
opportunity: Use regional harmonisation arguments to build a first formal framework.

name: Kenya
summary: Kenya’s Industry Guidelines for Child Online Protection are treated as binding on licensed ICT providers, even though the brief also notes the absence of a standalone COP law.
instruments: Industry Guidelines for Child Online Protection and Safety in Kenya
ODPC enforcement activity relating to children’s data
evidence: The brief treats Kenya as one of the strongest multi-layered systems while still arguing for consolidation into primary legislation.
opportunity: Support implementation and use Kenya to test what a future consolidated COP law should absorb.

name: Lesotho
summary: Lesotho is named as having COP Guidelines from 2024, but not primary legislative backing.
evidence: The article uses Lesotho to show that guideline growth does not equal legislative permanence.
opportunity: Use the guideline base as a launch point for stronger legal consolidation.

name: Libya
summary: The brief identifies Libya as having no identifiable child online safety framework in any form.
instruments: No identifiable framework named in the brief
evidence: Libya is explicitly listed in the article’s no-framework category.
opportunity: Establish baseline policy framing before debating design details.

name: Malawi
summary: Malawi is cited as having a draft strategy and a MACRA COP initiative, but no tangible legislative backing yet.
evidence: The brief places Malawi alongside Zambia and Lesotho in the strategy-first category.
opportunity: Advance from strategy language to enforceable obligations and implementation mechanisms.

name: Mali
summary: Mali’s 2024 children’s data directive is cited as an example of DPA-led movement in Francophone Africa before dedicated COP legislation exists.
evidence: The brief uses Mali both in the Francophone acceleration pattern and as a strategic DPA-entry-point precedent.
opportunity: Push children’s data rules further into design obligations, transparency, and profiling limits.

name: Mauritius
summary: Mauritius is identified as having a telecommunications directive implementing binding child online protection measures in 2025.
evidence: The brief places Mauritius inside the enacted or binding-instrument category.
opportunity: Surface Mauritius as a model for regulator-led implementation within licensed provider ecosystems.

name: Morocco
summary: Morocco is named among the significant digital economies that still rely on broader cybercrime, penal, or data protection law rather than dedicated COP legislation.
instruments: General cybercrime, penal, or data protection coverage referenced in the brief
evidence: The article explicitly includes Morocco in the general-law category rather than the dedicated COP categories.
opportunity: Use Morocco as a North African domestication target for dedicated child online protection obligations.

name: Nigeria
summary: The brief identifies Nigeria’s Child Online Access Protection Bill HB.244 as having passed the House in December 2025 and awaiting Senate action, while noting Nigeria still lacks standalone COP law.
instruments: Child Online Access Protection Bill HB.244
evidence: Nigeria is the brief’s clearest high-impact and high-feasibility advocacy target.
opportunity: Push the bill through the Senate and connect it to implementation architecture early.

name: Rwanda
summary: Rwanda is described as having a ministerial order, a five-year action plan, and primary COP legislation reportedly under consideration, with Budapest Convention accession in January 2025.
instruments: Ministerial Order on Child Online Protection 2024
evidence: The brief treats Rwanda as part of the next legislative tier behind current binding leaders.
opportunity: Support finalisation of primary legislation while protecting the rights-respecting design the article calls for.

name: Senegal
summary: Senegal is used as a key example of data protection authorities issuing children’s data directives in 2024.
evidence: The article highlights Senegal as proof that data protection authorities can become practical COP actors before dedicated laws exist.
opportunity: Expand DPA-led rule-setting as a bridge toward stronger national frameworks.

name: South Africa
summary: South Africa is described as having an advanced but fragmented framework and still lacking dedicated standalone COP legislation.
instruments: Information Regulator participation in age-assurance initiatives
evidence: The brief argues South Africa is strong in substance but still vulnerable because protections remain fragmented.
opportunity: Advocate for a standalone law that consolidates existing protections into a child-specific framework.

name: Tanzania
summary: The article highlights Tanzania’s 2024 Child Protection Laws Miscellaneous Amendments Act as a core acceleration point.
instruments: Child Protection Laws Miscellaneous Amendments Act 2024
evidence: Tanzania is used in the brief as a leading example of dedicated legislative movement during 2023 to 2025.
opportunity: Position Tanzania as evidence that targeted statutory amendments can move faster than entirely new frameworks.

name: Uganda
summary: Uganda is cited both as a significant digital economy in the general-law category and as a warning that poorly designed online safety law can trigger constitutional and rights concerns.
evidence: The article references a Constitutional Court decision from March 2026 striking down several sections of the Computer Misuse (Amendment) Act 2022.
opportunity: Use Uganda to insist on proportionality, scope limits, and explicit safeguards in future COP legislation.

name: Zambia
summary: Zambia launched a National Child Online Protection Strategy for 2025 to 2029 without corresponding primary legislation.
evidence: The brief treats Zambia as significant movement, but still below the threshold of durable legislative coverage.
opportunity: Support implementation while advocating legislative anchoring.

name: Zimbabwe
summary: Zimbabwe is identified as having a draft Child Online Protection Policy under development.
evidence: The article places Zimbabwe in the draft-policy category rather than the enacted or strategy-only categories.
opportunity: Push for a transition from policy drafting to legal and institutional backing.
